texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the last decades, gas-liquid membrane contactor systems have been studied extensively as an alternative way of contacting gas and liquid. The removal of acid gas (CO2) using aqueous amine solution is a particularly interesting case [1-26]. The main advantage of membrane contactors over conventional packed (random or structured) columns is that the non-wetted membrane can be considered as a well-defined gas-liquid interfacial area. Separation of the two phases by the membrane leads to the prevention of flooding and foaming of the absorbent liquid (solvent). In this hybrid process, the overall selectivity is still determined by significant differences in the absorption of different gas components in the selected solvent, which makes it possible to apply even non-selective (e.g. microporous) membranes. texts.label: text
texts.orig: This work deals with the study of chemical stability and the general behavior of hydrophobic glassy polymers with an excess of free volume fraction, such as poly[1-(trimethylsylil)-1-propyne] (PTMSP), poly[1-(trimethylgermil)-1-propyne] (PTMGP) and poly[4-methyl-2-pentyne] (PMP). The dense membranes based on these polymers have been tested at conditions which are typical for the regeneration of CO2 absorbent liquids (e.g. CCS). As expected, these membrane materials initially showed a decline of the gas permeability (N2, O2 and CO2) at 100 ◦ C, which is mainly attributed to the relaxation of the free volume structure. The transport parameters then leveled off and a slight decreasing of the gas transport parameters in time was observed. In contrast to previous published data, no chemical degradation of those polymers, in particular the absence of C O and C-O groups, was detected by FTIR spectroscopy, even after exposure to air at 150 ◦ C. In addition, long-term contact with different kinds of physical and chemical absorbent liquids at 100 ◦ C did not lead to changes in the chemical structure and macroscopic properties of those polymers. It was shown that absorption liquids like water or alkanolamines can be regenerated by pressure and temperature swing ( /Delta1 pm = 40 bar, T = 100 ◦ C) in gas-liquid contactors based on PTMSP, PTMGP and PMP.
texts.text: This work deals with the study of chemical stability and the general behavior of hydrophobic glassy polymers with an excess of free volume fraction, such as poly[1-(trimethylsylil)-1-propyne] (PTMSP), poly[1-(trimethylgermil)-1-propyne] (PTMGP) and poly[4-methyl-2-pentyne] (PMP). The dense membranes based on these polymers have been tested at conditions which are typical for the regeneration of CO2 absorbent liquids (e.g. CCS). As expected, these membrane materials initially showed a decline of the gas permeability (N2, O2 and CO2) at 100 ◦ C, which is mainly attributed to the relaxation of the free volume structure. The transport parameters then leveled off and a slight decreasing of the gas transport parameters in time was observed. In contrast to previous published data, no chemical degradation of those polymers, in particular the absence of C O and C-O groups, was detected by FTIR spectroscopy, even after exposure to air at 150 ◦ C. In addition, long-term contact with different kinds of physical and chemical absorbent liquids at 100 ◦ C did not lead to changes in the chemical structure and macroscopic properties of those polymers. It was shown that absorption liquids like water or alkanolamines can be regenerated by pressure and temperature swing ( /Delta1 pm = 40 bar, T = 100 ◦ C) in gas-liquid contactors based on PTMSP, PTMGP and PMP.

texts.orig: Analysis of the literature shows that most of the studies in this area are focusing on the carbon dioxide absorption process by using hollow fiber microporous membranes based on hydrophobic polymers such as poly(propylene) (PP), poly(ethylene) (PE), poly(tetrafluoroethylene) (PTFE) and poly(vinylidene fluoride) (PVDF) [1-11,18-20,24,25]. These membranes, which are usually commercially available, provide high membrane packing and low mass transfer resistance due to high membrane porosity, up to 80%. On the other hand, such microporous structures (average pore size can vary in the range of 0.03-0.60 𝛍 m) can lead to significant solvent losses due to evaporation, especially at higher temperatures. In addition, even partial wetting of the membrane pores during the operation dramatically increases the membrane resistance, resulting in decreases in the overall mass transfer coefficients in comparison with the non-wetted mode of operation [4,5,9,18-20,24,25]. texts.orig: At moderate operating conditions, applications of membrane contactors have been extensively studied and the cheaper porous membranes (already available in the market for other applications) have been the prime focus. However, it was already shown that membranes with a dense selective layer could have certain advantages over porous ones for operation at severe conditions (temperature, pressure and solvents):
texts.text: At moderate operating conditions, applications of membrane contactors have been extensively studied and the cheaper porous membranes (already available in the market for other applications) have been the prime focus. However, it was already shown that membranes with a dense selective layer could have certain advantages over porous ones for operation at severe conditions (temperature, pressure and solvents):

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: i) Absorption liquid regeneration at high temperatures. A comparison of the performance of two types of flatsheet membranes, commercial porous PTFE and asymmetric poly(vinyltrimethylsilane) (PVTMS), has revealed that a dense selective layer of PVTMS could significantly reduce evaporation during the regeneration of monoethanolamine-based absorption liquid at temperatures up to 110 ◦ C [23]. The resistance over the dense selective layer did not lead to a significant mass transfer reduction; both membranes had a comparable CO2 flux at the same operating conditions.
texts.text: Absorption liquid regeneration at high temperatures. A comparison of the performance of two types of flatsheet membranes, commercial porous PTFE and asymmetric poly(vinyltrimethylsilane) (PVTMS), has revealed that a dense selective layer of PVTMS could significantly reduce evaporation during the regeneration of monoethanolamine-based absorption liquid at temperatures up to 110 ◦ C [23]. The resistance over the dense selective layer did not lead to a significant mass transfer reduction; both membranes had a comparable CO2 flux at the same operating conditions.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: ii) Absorption/desorption process with a high pressure difference over the membrane (flexibility in operation of gas and liquid pressures). It was already claimed [17] that membranes with a selective layer based on the most permeable polymer poly[1-(trimethylsilyl)-1-propyne] (PTMSP) show barrier properties towards different types of absorbents at trans-membrane pressure up to 40 bar, while CO2 permeates freely through the membrane.
texts.text: ii) Absorption/desorption process with a high pressure difference over the membrane (flexibility in operation of gas and liquid pressures). It was already claimed [17] that membranes with a selective layer based on the most permeable polymer poly[1-(trimethylsilyl)-1-propyne] (PTMSP) show barrier properties towards different types of absorbents at trans-membrane pressure up to 40 bar, while CO2 permeates freely through the membrane.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To minimize the additional resistance of the dense layer, the polymers used should have a high CO2 flux. Obviously, hydrophobic glassy polymers ( Tg > 250 ◦ C) with an extremely high free volume fraction (more than 20%) have been considered [10,11,17,26] as perspective membrane materials for gas-liquid contactors. These polymers have one of the highest gas permeabilities among known polymers and good mechanical properties [27,28]. On the other hand, it was already reported [28-36] that these polymers are hampered with a decline of the gas transport properties in time. This can be attributed to physical ageing (relaxation of non-equilibrium free volume), chemical ageing (chemical degradation of the polymer), and/or contamination (adsorption of molecules in the pores).
texts.text: To minimize the additional resistance of the dense layer, the polymers used should have a high CO2 flux. Obviously, hydrophobic glassy polymers ( Tg > 250 ◦ C) with an extremely high free volume fraction (more than 20%) have been considered [10,11,17,26] as perspective membrane materials for gas-liquid contactors. These polymers have one of the highest gas permeabilities among known polymers and good mechanical properties [27,28]. On the other hand, it was already reported [28-36] that these polymers are hampered with a decline of the gas transport properties in time. This can be attributed to physical ageing (relaxation of non-equilibrium free volume), chemical ageing (chemical degradation of the polymer), and/or contamination (adsorption of molecules in the pores).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: It is critical for successful long term operation of gas-liquid membrane contactors that the membrane material is chemically stable in the presence of absorption liquids at the relevant operating conditions [9,24] as well as having good barrier properties towards the absorption liquid at high trans-membrane pressure. Unfortunately, there is a lack of information in the literature regarding stability of these membrane materials, leakage of absorbents through the membrane, and operation at high pressure and temperature. Therefore, the aim of this work was to study extensively the chemical stability and other related properties of membrane materials based on high permeability polyacetylenes, such as poly[1-(trimethylsylil)-1-propyne] (PTMSP), poly[1-(trimethylgermil)-1-propyne] (PTMGP) and poly[4-methyl2-pentyne] (PMP). The focus of this work is to examine the above mentioned materials for the regeneration of amine-based absorption liquids at high pressure and temperature. Based on this work, the suitability of these membranes for membrane gas desorption (MGD) can be assessed.

texts.orig: For this study, three high permeability glassy polymers were selected, see Table 1. All of these polymers are in the glassy state, even at the operation temperatures needed for the absorption liquid regeneration process, and have high values for the CO2 permeability coefficient at 25 ◦ C.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: PTMSP (NbCl5), PTMSP (TaCl5/TIBA), PTMGP and PMP were synthesized according to the techniques developed at TIPS RAS. PTMSP was synthesized in toluene solution using NbCl5 and TaCl5 with cocatalyst triisobutylaluminum (TIBA) as the catalyst; PTMGP was synthesized using TaCl5 [37]. Polymerizations were carried out under the following conditions: [Monomer]/[Catalyst] = 50, [Cocatalyst]/[Catalyst] = 0.3, [Monomer]0 = 1 mol/l, T = 25 ◦ C. PMP was synthesized in cyclohexane solution with the catalytic system NbCl5/Et3SiH [38]. Polymerization was carried out at the following conditions: [Monomer]/[Catalyst] = 50, [Cocatalyst]/[Catalyst] = 1, [Monomer]0 = 1 mol/l, T = 25 ◦ C.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.2. Membrane preparation
texts.text: 2.2. Membrane preparation

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The dense membranes were cast from a cyclohexane solution (polymer concentration: 0.5-3.0 wt.%) onto commercial cellophane. The cast film was then covered with a Petri dish and left for slow evaporation for several days followed by drying in the oven at 40 ◦ C until reaching a constant sample weight. After casting, the membrane thickness was measured by an electronic micrometer (Mitutoyo ® ) and was in the range of 20-40 𝛍 m. Further treatment of all membranes was according to standard protocol of membrane preparation [39].

Further treatment of all membranes was according to standard protocol of membrane preparation [39].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To investigate the chemical stability of the selected membrane materials, a number of commercially available physical and chemical absorbent liquids were chosen. The Genosorb 300 and Genosorb 1753 (polyethylene glycol dimethyl ether) were provided by Clariant Produkte GmbH via TNO. The 2-(2-aminoethylamino)ethanol (AEAE) was supplied by 'Kazanorgsyntez' (Republic of Tatarstan, Russian Federation). The propylene carbonate (PC), N-methyl2-pyrrolidone (NMP), monoethanolamine (MEA), diethanolamine (DEA), N-methyldiethanolamine (MDEA), 2-amino-2-methyl-1propanol (AMP), 2-diethylaminoethanol (DEAE) and piperazine (PZ) with purity of at least 99% were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Chemie Gmbh. All of these liquids were used without any further purification. Distilled water was used as a physical absorbent and for the preparation of aqueous solutions of MEA (30 wt.%), DEA (30 wt.%), MDEA (30 wt.%), AMP (30 wt.%), DEAE (30 wt.%), AEAE (30 wt.%), and PZ (2.5 M).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The changes of chemical structure of the membranes were investigated by FTIR spectroscopy, IFS-66 v/s vacuum Bruker, with recording of IR spectra in the pass mode in the range of 4000-400 cm -1 and data processing using the software OPUS 6.0 (Bruker). The pretreatment of the samples before infrared characterization was the following: the membrane samples were soaked in ethanol for 4 days at room temperature after exposure to the absorption liquid to extract the remaining absorption liquid; the ethanol was then subsequently replaced by distilled water by decreasing the alcohol concentration step by step; and, finally, the membranes were soaked in distilled water for 1 day with drying in the oven at 40 ◦ C for 2 days.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The density and microporosity of the dense membranes were measured using the flotation method described elsewhere [40]. The principle of the method is based on comparing sample weights in air, water (non-wetting liquid), and ethanol (wetting liquid). Using non-wetting liquids, a geometric density /SUB g , which is the density of the membrane sample as a whole, is measured. With wetting liquids, a pycnometric density /SUB p is measured. This density is related to the densest regions of polymer with a high excess of free-volume. texts.orig: 2.7. Membrane leakage testing
texts.text: 2.7. Membrane leakage testing

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Absorption liquid leakage testing of the flat-sheet membranes was carried out in a dead-end filtration cell at a pressure of 40 bar and temperature of 100 ◦ C. The membrane was placed into the cell and the upper compartment of the cell was partially filled with the selected absorption fluid (total liquid volume was about 75 cm 3 ), leading to a stagnant liquid height of 4 cm. To avoid damage to the membrane due to the high pressure, a porous stainless steel support was used. The chamber above the liquid was pressurized using pure CO2. The glass collector for the permeated absorption liquid was kept at the same temperature as the membrane cell to avoid possible vapor condensation.

texts.orig: 2.8. Membrane gas desorption at high pressure and temperature
texts.text: 2.8. Membrane gas desorption at high pressure and temperature

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The membrane gas desorption process based on the selected dense membranes was studied using the set-up presented in Fig. 1. This setup was operated in a continuous mode. As a representative absorption liquid a 30 wt.% diethanolamine (DEA) was chosen. This absorption liquid was saturated with CO2 in absorber 3 at 10 bar and 30 ◦ C (thermostat 5 and oven 6 ). The absorber 3 was pressurized with pure CO2 at the required pressure during the experiment to maintain the overall liquid pressure in the system. The liquid velocity was controlled with the needle valve 11 at the testing system outlet. The saturated liquid was fed into membrane gas-liquid contactor 8 at 100 ◦ C (oven 6 ), where the CO2 desorption process from the absorption liquid through the membrane could take place; no vacuum or stripping gas was used to facilitate CO2 transport through the membrane. The membrane contactor consisted of a stainless steel gas-liquid contactor 8 with an active membrane surface area of 16.6 cm 2 . The CO2 flux through the membrane was measured using flow meter 15 . The thickness of the liquid layer in the membrane contactor was 0.1 mm. O-rings stable in water/DEA media were used to seal the contactor and the membrane. A porous stainless steel support with low gas flow resistance was used in the gas compartment of the cell. A stainless steel pipe (Swagelok, OD = 3 mm) with a length of about 1.5 m was installed in the oven 6 , just before the membrane cell 8 , as a heat exchanger.

texts.orig: Initially, the permeability of gases, such as O2, N2 and CO2, through the dense membranes based on PTMSP (NbCl5), PTMSP (TaCl5/TIBA), PTMGP, and PMP was measured at trans-membrane pressures up to 40 bar and room temperature. As expected, Table 2 reveals that the chosen glassy polymers have one of the highest gas permeabilities among known polymers [27]. This is due to the extremely high non-equilibrium free volume fraction: for example, PTMSP -up to 34% [35], PMP -22% [31]. Other polymers such as poly(dimethylsiloxan) (PDMS), PVTMS, and poly(phenylene oxide) (PPO), which are also proposed as membrane materials for the dense layer [12,13,15,16,21,22], have much lower CO2 permeability values -3230, 190 and 57 Barrer, respectively [41].

texts.orig: It is obvious that for membrane gas desorption, the selected membranes should be stable at high temperature (e.g. about 100 ◦ C) while exposed to the absorption liquid. Therefore, chemical resistance of the membrane towards the absorption liquid is crucial for the development of membrane gas desorption. Thus, for the evaluation of mutual compatibility of the absorption liquid and the membrane, the membrane samples listed in Table 1 were immersed in the different absorption liquids (see Section 2.3) with a total time of exposure of 300 h at a temperature of 100 ◦ C. However, it should be noted that the total duration of contact of the membranes with the different solvents was at least 3 times higher with respect to keeping at room temperature overnight and during holidays.

A pressure of 10 bar was chosen to mimic the partial pressure of CO2 in the gas stream after water shift reaction (CO2:H2 = 1:3; P = 34-40 bar). The loaded DEA solution was heated to 100 ◦ C. Subsequently, the CO2 flux during the membrane gas desorption step was measured as a function of flow rate of solvent in the membrane contactor.

texts.orig: In summary, it can be expected that the membranes based on one of the considered glassy polymers should offer sufficient chemical and mechanical stability required for long-term operation in membrane gas desorption systems at elevated temperatures and pressures. However, the application for the membrane gas absorption process can still be limited by the feed stream composition. Items to be considered are, for example, the effect of hydrocarbons and aromatic compounds on the swelling degree of the membrane and even partial dissolution of the membrane material.

texts.orig: 4. Conclusions
texts.text: 4. Conclusions

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this work, the chemical stability and general behavior of the high permeability glassy polymers with an excess of free volume fraction (more than 20%); such as PTMSP, PTMGP and PMP; have been extensively studied at conditions which are typical for the regeneration of physical and chemical solvents in acid gas removal processes. This can be relevant for sour gas treatment and CO2 preand post-combustion capture. As expected from the authors' own experience and literature data, these membrane materials initially show a decline of the gas permeability (N2, O2 and CO2) at 100 ◦ C. This is mainly attributed to the relaxation of the free volume structure. After this initial period, only a slight decrease of the gas transport parameters was observed over time. In contrast to previously published data, no chemical degradation of those polymers, in particular the absence of C O and C-O groups, was detected by FTIR spectroscopy, even at more severe exposure conditions (air at 150 ◦ C). Moreover, long-term contact with different kinds of physical and chemical absorbents at 100 ◦ C does not lead to changes of the chemical structure and macroscopic properties of those polymers. Therefore, it can be concluded that the high permeability glassy polymers, which are already considered as very interesting membrane materials for a wide range of applications, can be operated in the membrane gas absorption/desorption process. Despite physical ageing in time, PTMSP, PTMGP and PMP still showed one of the highest CO2 permeabilities, even after partial relaxation of the porous polymer structure.
texts.text: In this work, the chemical stability and general behavior of the high permeability glassy polymers with an excess of free volume fraction (more than 20%); such as PTMSP, PTMGP and PMP; have been extensively studied at conditions which are typical for the regeneration of physical and chemical solvents in acid gas removal processes. This can be relevant for sour gas treatment and CO2 preand post-combustion capture. As expected from the authors' own experience and literature data, these membrane materials initially show a decline of the gas permeability (N2, O2 and CO2) at 100 ◦ C. This is mainly attributed to the relaxation of the free volume structure. After this initial period, only a slight decrease of the gas transport parameters was observed over time. In contrast to previously published data, no chemical degradation of those polymers, in particular the absence of C O and C-O groups, was detected by FTIR spectroscopy, even at more severe exposure conditions (air at 150 ◦ C). Moreover, long-term contact with different kinds of physical and chemical absorbents at 100 ◦ C does not lead to changes of the chemical structure and macroscopic properties of those polymers. Therefore, it can be concluded that the high permeability glassy polymers, which are already considered as very interesting membrane materials for a wide range of applications, can be operated in the membrane gas absorption/desorption process. Despite physical ageing in time, PTMSP, PTMGP and PMP still showed one of the highest CO2 permeabilities, even after partial relaxation of the porous polymer structure.
